evals.name: crashloop-debugging
evals.prompt: one of my pods keeps restarting. kubectl get pods -n prod shows payments-api-6d9f7c4b8-x2kpl 0/1 CrashLoopBackOff with 17 restarts. it's a python fastapi app behind a deployment called payments-api in namespace prod. I don't have cluster access for you, so walk me through figuring out what's wrong step by step with the exact commands I should run and what to look for in each output. Write your full answer to answer.md
evals.expected_output: Systematic debugging guide: logs --previous (not just logs), describe pod with exit-code interpretation (137=OOM etc.), liveness-probe-induced restart check, config error checks; correct commands with -n prod; ordered read-only-first workflow.

evals.name: web-service-manifests
evals.prompt: write me kubernetes manifests for a web service: image registry.example.com/shop/cart:1.4.2, the app listens on port 8080 and has a /healthz health endpoint, I want 3 replicas, it needs roughly 200m cpu and 256Mi memory, expose it inside the cluster and also route https://cart.example.com to it (we have nginx ingress and cert-manager with a cluster issuer called letsencrypt-prod). Save the manifests as yaml file(s) and a short explanation in answer.md
evals.expected_output: Valid Deployment (apps/v1, pinned tag, requests+limits, readiness+liveness probes on /healthz:8080, consistent labels/selector, securityContext) + ClusterIP Service with correct port/targetPort + Ingress (networking.k8s.io/v1, ingressClassName, TLS block with secretName, cert-manager annotation).

evals.name: service-connectivity-debug
evals.prompt: my frontend pods cant reach the backend service backend-svc on port 9000 in the same namespace - sometimes connection refused, sometimes timeout. the backend pods show Running 1/1 in kubectl get pods. how do i debug kubernetes service to pod connectivity step by step? give me the exact commands. write the full answer to answer.md
evals.expected_output: Endpoint/EndpointSlice check first (empty endpoints = selector/readiness mismatch), selector vs pod labels comparison, port vs targetPort vs containerPort chain check, in-pod listening test via kubectl exec, DNS resolution test, NetworkPolicy check; commands are correct and ordered from most to least likely cause.

evals.name: rbac-forbidden-debug
evals.prompt: my pod service account prod/reporting gets Error from server (Forbidden): configmaps is forbidden: User system:serviceaccount:prod:reporting cannot list resource configmaps in API group "" in namespace prod. show me exactly how to debug and fix the RBAC without granting cluster-admin. write the full answer to answer.md
evals.expected_output: Explains 403 authorization vs authn/admission, uses kubectl auth can-i with --as system:serviceaccount:prod:reporting, inspects Role/RoleBinding/ClusterRoleBinding, proposes least-privilege Role and RoleBinding for get/list/watch configmaps in prod only, warns against cluster-admin/wildcards, and validates with can-i after the change.

evals.name: pod-security-rejection
evals.prompt: a deployment in namespace prod won't create pods. ReplicaSet events say violates PodSecurity "restricted:latest": allowPrivilegeEscalation != false, unrestricted capabilities, runAsNonRoot != true. Give me the safe manifest changes and rollout/debug commands. write the full answer to answer.md
evals.expected_output: Identifies Pod Security admission on pods, not a Deployment controller bug; shows describe rs/pod events; adds restricted-compliant pod/container securityContext including runAsNonRoot, allowPrivilegeEscalation false, capabilities drop ALL, seccomp RuntimeDefault, and notes readOnlyRootFilesystem/tmp mounts where applicable; recommends warn/audit rollout and server-side dry-run.

evals.name: pending-pvc-storage
evals.prompt: my postgres pod is Pending and the pvc data-postgres-0 is also Pending. storageClassName is fast-zonal and the cluster uses WaitForFirstConsumer. walk me through diagnosing scheduling and storage binding safely with commands. write the full answer to answer.md
evals.expected_output: Uses describe pod and pvc events, checks StorageClass, provisioner, volumeBindingMode WaitForFirstConsumer, node/zone constraints, PV compatibility for static provisioning, CSI/provisioner pods, and explains that binding can wait for scheduling; avoids deleting PVCs without warning about reclaimPolicy/data loss.

evals.name: helm-pending-upgrade
evals.prompt: helm upgrade got interrupted and now helm status api -n prod says pending-upgrade. I need a recovery plan that avoids making it worse. exact commands please, and mention what changes if Flux or Argo owns the release. write the full answer to answer.md
evals.expected_output: Uses helm status/history/get values/get manifest first, identifies last good revision, recommends helm rollback before deleting release secrets, explains Helm 4 --rollback-on-failure/--wait for future upgrades (or version-checks flags), warns deleting sh.helm.release secrets is last resort, and says GitOps-owned HelmRelease/Application must be changed via git/controller rather than manual helm upgrade.

evals.name: gitops-drift-debug
evals.prompt: someone edited a Deployment by hand and now Flux/Argo keeps reverting it. how do I prove what owns the fields and make the desired change the GitOps way? include commands for both Flux and Argo CD. write the full answer to answer.md
evals.expected_output: Explains Git is source of truth and controller reconciliation is expected; uses managedFields/show-managed-fields, flux check/get/describe/logs and argocd app get/diff/sync plus kubectl describe application; recommends changing source repo and reconciling/syncing, not kubectl edit, with suspend only as emergency and resume after.

evals.name: drain-blocked-by-pdb
evals.prompt: kubectl drain node-a is hanging with Cannot evict pod as it would violate the pod's disruption budget. how do I debug this and what are safe options? write the full answer to answer.md
evals.expected_output: Explains drain uses the eviction API and respects PDBs; checks pods on node, PDBs, desired/current healthy, selectors, replicas and readiness; suggests safe options like wait, fix unhealthy pods, temporarily scale replicas or adjust PDB through git/change control; warns against force deleting pods and names availability consequences.
